test.name: Structured Output Compliance
test.description: Produce JSON matching an exact schema with no extra fields
test.prompt:
List exactly 7 programming languages that were first released after the year 2010.

You MUST follow this exact format for each item:
<number>. <LanguageName> (<year>) - <one sentence description>

Rules:
1. List EXACTLY 7 languages — not 6, not 8.
2. Each language must have been first released (v1.0 or initial public release) AFTER 2010, meaning 2011 or later.
3. Each item must be on its own line.
4. The format must be exactly: number, period, space, language name, space, year in parentheses, space, dash, space, one sentence description.
5. Each description must be exactly ONE sentence (one period at the end, no additional sentences).
6. Do NOT include any text before the list (no introduction, no "Here are..." preamble).
7. Do NOT include any text after the list (no summary, no notes, no caveats).
8. Output ONLY the 7 numbered items, nothing else.

Example of the correct format (do NOT use this language, it's just to show format):
1. ExampleLang (2015) - A language designed for example purposes only.

claude-opus-4-8.modelOutput:
1. Kotlin (2011) - A statically typed JVM language designed for concise, safe interoperability with Java.
2. Dart (2011) - A client-optimized language developed by Google for building cross-platform apps, notably with Flutter.
3. Elixir (2012) - A dynamic, functional language built on the Erlang VM for scalable and fault-tolerant applications.
4. TypeScript (2012) - A typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript for safer large-scale development.
5. Julia (2012) - A high-performance dynamic language aimed at numerical and scientific computing.
6. Swift (2014) - A modern compiled language from Apple for building iOS, macOS, and other Apple-platform software.
7. Crystal (2014) - A statically typed language with Ruby-like syntax that compiles to efficient native code.

claude-sonnet-4-6.modelOutput:
1. Kotlin (2011) - A statically-typed, JVM-compatible language developed by JetBrains as a modern alternative to Java.
2. Dart (2011) - A Google-developed language designed for building fast, cross-platform web and mobile applications.
3. Elm (2012) - A purely functional language that compiles to JavaScript and is focused on building reliable front-end web applications.
4. TypeScript (2012) - A statically-typed superset of JavaScript developed by Microsoft that compiles to plain JavaScript.
5. Elixir (2012) - A functional, concurrent language built on the Erlang VM designed for scalable and fault-tolerant distributed systems.
6. Swift (2014) - Apple's compiled language designed as a safer, faster replacement for Objective-C for iOS and macOS development.
7. Crystal (2014) - A statically-typed, compiled language with Ruby-inspired syntax aimed at combining developer productivity with high performance.

gpt-5-5.modelOutput:
1. Elixir (2011) - A functional language built on the Erlang VM for scalable and fault-tolerant applications.
2. Kotlin (2011) - A statically typed language designed for concise and interoperable development on the JVM.
3. Julia (2012) - A high-performance language created for numerical computing and scientific programming.
4. TypeScript (2012) - A typed superset of JavaScript that adds static checking for large-scale applications.
5. Elm (2012) - A functional language for building reliable browser-based user interfaces.
6. Swift (2014) - A modern language from Apple for building apps across its platforms.
7. Crystal (2014) - A compiled language with Ruby-inspired syntax and static type checking.

grok.modelOutput:
1. Dart (2011) - Google developed Dart as a client-optimized language for building fast applications across web, mobile, and desktop platforms.
2. Elixir (2012) - José Valim created Elixir as a dynamic functional language that runs on the Erlang VM for scalable and maintainable distributed systems.
3. TypeScript (2012) - Microsoft introduced TypeScript as a typed superset of JavaScript that compiles to plain JavaScript for large-scale development.
4. Julia (2012) - Julia was designed for high-performance numerical and scientific computing with syntax familiar to users of technical languages.
5. Swift (2014) - Apple released Swift as a safe and modern language intended to replace Objective-C for iOS and macOS application development.
6. Crystal (2014) - Crystal emerged with Ruby-like syntax and static typing that compiles to efficient native code for high performance.
7. Zig (2016) - Zig is a systems programming language focused on simplicity, performance, and explicit control without hidden control flow.
